topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Arthur meticulously polished his antique compass, the brass gleaming under the weak kitchen light. He felt a familiar tightening in his chest, a stubborn refusal to yield to the chaos swirling around him. His daughter, Lily, now spent hours crouched on the floor, tracing imaginary lines on the worn rug, muttering about magnetic north and hidden treasure. "It's not just a toy, Dad," she’d declared yesterday, her eyes wide with a fierce, unwavering certainty. "It's the key to everything." Arthur just sighed, the sound heavy with exhaustion. He picked up the compass, the cool metal grounding him, a small island of order in the tidal wave of her intense focus. He resisted the urge to simply toss the object aside.

[story 2]

"Stop touching my workbench, Leo!" I snapped, my voice sharper than I intended. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles white against the familiar wood grain of the carving tools. My grandfather’s old woodworking hobby, the precise shaping of oak, had become Lily’s singular fixation. She’d cornered me in the garage, her small face set in a look of absolute command. "You're not going to let me finish the birdhouse, Grandpa. It needs to be perfect. Exactly like the blueprints." I stared at the half-finished piece, the grain mocking my attempts at control. A hot flush crept up my neck. I wanted to shout, to tell her that perfection wasn't a cage, but I swallowed the words. Instead, I simply stepped back, letting the silence speak volumes about my unwillingness to participate in this new, demanding reality.

[story 3]

The scent of turpentine and linseed oil usually brought me peace, a quiet sanctuary where my old passion, painting landscapes, reigned supreme. Now, that sanctuary felt invaded. Lily had somehow convinced me that every brushstroke I made was merely a placeholder until the real masterpiece—a sprawling, impossibly detailed mural of constellations—was complete. I watched her, her brow furrowed in concentration, her small hand gripping a palette knife with surprising strength. I felt a distinct stiffness in my shoulders, a physical resistance to the direction she was pulling me. "It doesn't have to be that big, sweetie," I managed, my tone flat, devoid of enthusiasm. She merely looked up, her expression utterly unyielding, a mirror reflecting my own internal struggle against the sudden, overwhelming need to give in to her vision.
